projects.name: Solar + Energy Storage on Garage
projects.description: Install 5-7 solar panels on south-facing garage roof face (20x12 ft, ~2-2.8 kW) plus battery storage. Solar = standalone ROI investment (~5yr payback at $0.42/kWh). Battery = generator alternative (no lot space for generator). 30% federal ITC applies to both when paired.
projects.notes: DESIGN: South garage face only. 5 portrait panels = 2.0 kW; 7 mixed = 2.8 kW. ~3,200-4,500 kWh/yr production (20-26% offset). All-in after ITC: solar ~$9-10k, battery ~$9-10k. VENDORS: Sunergy (sunergy.com) — EnergySage 2026 CA Installer of Year, Enphase Platinum, no door-to-door. NRG Clean Power (nrgcleanpower.com) — Diamond Certified, 35yr, San Mateo County, 40yr warranty. Tesla Energy (tesla.com/energy) — online only. AVOID: Sunrun.

tasks.title: Analyze energy usage and size system
tasks.notes: 17,178 kWh/yr at $0.42/kWh avg. Target: 5-7 panels south garage face (20x12 ft) = 2-2.8 kW. ~5yr payback after ITC.

tasks.title: Research and shortlist solar installers
tasks.notes: Shortlist: Sunergy (sunergy.com, Enphase Platinum, no door-to-door), NRG Clean Power (nrgcleanpower.com, Diamond Certified, San Mateo County), Tesla Energy (tesla.com/energy, online only). Avoid Sunrun.

tasks.title: Contact installers for quotes
tasks.notes: Spec: south garage face (20x12 ft, 5-7 panels, ~2-2.8 kW), paired battery (Enphase IQ or Powerwall 3), detached garage sub-panel interconnect. Ask each for: $/W, panel brand, inverter type, battery spec, warranty, timeline, ITC total. Request free shade/LIDAR analysis.

tasks.title: Compare quotes and select installer
tasks.notes: Prefer Enphase microinverters over string inverter. Compare $/W, battery spec, warranty, timeline. 30% ITC on full system incl. battery.

tasks.title: Confirm ITC timing — credit files in year PTO is granted
tasks.notes: 30% federal ITC on Form 5695. Claimed in tax year Permission to Operate granted by PG&E. Battery qualifies only when solar-paired. Plan install timing accordingly.

observations.summary: speech detected at backyard
dog barking at front_yard
dog barking at front_door
dog barking at entryway
speech detected at living_room
speech detected at dog_run

The audio likely contains dog barks recorded at the front door. — The rule guess suggests 'bark' with a confidence of 0.72, and the mid-band energy is high, which is characteristic of barks or speech. The spectrogram shows intermittent energy bursts consistent with barking.

The audio appears to contain dog barks, consistent with the presence of two Australian Shepherd dogs in the entryway area. — The high Mid-band energy (0.798) and the rule guess pointing to 'bark' support this classification. The spectrogram shows intermittent, sharp energy bursts typical of barking.

The audio appears to contain human speech recorded in the living room. — The rule guess strongly suggests speech, supported by the mid-band energy concentration and spectral centroid within the typical speech range.

The audio contains moderate, intermittent energy consistent with general outdoor sounds, but no specific event is clearly identifiable. — The spectral centroid and mid-band energy suggest some vocalization or transient sounds, but the low energy levels and lack of distinct patterns make classification difficult.

The audio contains intermittent, moderate-level sounds consistent with general outdoor activity, but no distinct event is identifiable. — The energy distribution suggests some mid-to-high frequency activity, but the low energy in the low-band and the moderate peak/RMS ratio do not strongly point to a specific event like a bark or alarm.

The audio appears to contain intermittent bursts of sound consistent with dogs barking in the dog run area. — The high mid-band energy (0.672) and the peak/RMS ratio (3.05) suggest transient, non-continuous sounds, which aligns well with barking, especially given the presence of two dogs in the dog run.

The audio appears to contain speech sounds recorded in the living room. — The rule guess strongly suggests speech, supported by the mid-band energy concentration and spectral centroid within the typical speech range.

The audio appears to contain intermittent, moderate-frequency sounds consistent with general outdoor activity, but no specific event is clearly identifiable. — The mid-band energy is relatively high, suggesting some vocal or percussive sounds, but the low-band energy is low, and the spectral centroid is moderate, making a definitive classification difficult.
The audio contains intermittent bursts of sound in the front yard, but no distinct sound matches the known categories.

The audio appears to contain dog vocalizations consistent with barking, which is expected at the dog run location. — The high mid-band energy (0.730) and the peak/RMS ratio (4.47) suggest transient, non-wind noise, aligning with the 'bark' category, despite the rule guess favoring 'speech'.
The audio contains intermittent, low-level sounds consistent with general outdoor noise, but no specific event is clearly identifiable.

The audio appears to contain intermittent, low-energy sounds consistent with general background noise in the entryway. — The low overall energy and the spectral features (mid-band energy dominant, low ZCR) do not strongly match any specific event like a bark or doorbell ring, suggesting an unknown or very quiet ambient sound.

The audio appears to contain human speech recorded in the living room. — The rule guess strongly suggests speech, supported by the mid-band energy concentration and spectral centroid within the typical speech range.
unclassified audio at backyard

The doorbell ringing at 19:06, coupled with the dogs' increased activity (barking/alert sounds) and the general background noise, strongly suggests an unexpected visitor or delivery arrived while the family was settled in the living room. This is a clear, multi-signal event that indicates an immediate need for awareness.
